metadata.title: Build Your Website, Part 2: Make It Not Break on Mobile
metadata.playlist: Ship It — Build Your Brand's Live Presence
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 8 brief — Building Your Live Website, Resumes & LinkedIn Header

beats.narration_text: Hola — this is Liam. Episode 2. You have a draft from Part 1. Before it is done, you need to do one thing: open it on your phone. If it's broken on mobile, it is not done — because most of your visitors will see the mobile view before they ever see the desktop view. Then you publish and get the live link, because the live link is the actual deliverable — not the preview. Let's handle both.

props.segment: Build Your Website, Part 2: Make It Not Break on Mobile
props.command: Test on mobile. Fix the three things that always break. Publish. Get the live link.
output: Check mobile view — most visitors land here first.
Fix the three common break points before publishing.
The live URL is the deliverable — not the editor preview.

beats.narration_text: Responsive design means one codebase, multiple layouts. When your site is responsive, the layout reflows automatically based on the width of the screen it's displayed on. A desktop screen is roughly 1200 pixels wide, a tablet is around 768, and a phone is anywhere from 320 to 430. A responsive site detects that width and switches layouts — moving from a multi-column desktop grid to a single-column mobile stack, resizing text, and repositioning navigation. The AI platform you used in Part 1 generates responsive code by default, but it does not guarantee it works correctly — it gets the desktop right first and the mobile second, and the mobile often has errors you need to catch and fix.

artifactLines: Responsive: one codebase that reflows its layout based on screen width.
Desktop ≈ 1200 px wide — multi-column grid, full navigation bar.
Tablet ≈ 768 px wide — reduced columns, condensed navigation.
Mobile ≈ 320–430 px wide — single column, hamburger menu, scaled images.
AI platforms generate responsive code by default — but the mobile view often breaks anyway.
props.sparkLine: One codebase. Multiple layouts. Test all of them.

beats.narration_text: Every platform you might have chosen in Part 1 has a mobile preview toggle built into the editor. In v0.dev, look for a row of screen-size icons in the top toolbar — click the phone icon to switch the preview pane to mobile width. In Framer, the breakpoint switcher is in the top-right of the canvas, labeled with pixel dimensions — click the smallest one. In Wix, find the mobile editor toggle at the top of the screen, usually shown as a desktop and phone icon side by side. In Squarespace, use Edit in Full Preview mode and then resize your browser window, or find Device View in the toolbar. The exact label differs, but the pattern is the same across all four: look in the preview toolbar for a phone icon or a breakpoint toggle. Click it before you do anything else in this episode.

shot.new_visual_element: Mobile preview toggle illustrated generically across all four platforms: toolbar location, icon type (phone/breakpoint), and click target annotated for v0.dev, Framer, Wix, and Squarespace

platforms.name: v0.dev
platforms.trigger: Phone icon in screen-size icon row

platforms.name: Framer
platforms.trigger: Breakpoint switcher — select smallest px value

props.sparkLine: Look for a phone icon or a breakpoint toggle in the preview toolbar — every platform has one.

beats.act: the three things that break first
beats.narration_text: In a first-generation AI site, three things break on mobile more than anything else. Break point one: hero text overflows off-screen. The heading — your name and role — is sized for a 1200-pixel desktop viewport. On a 390-pixel phone screen, it extends past the right edge. Visitors have to scroll sideways to read it, and most won't. Break point two: images stay fixed-width and overflow. A project image set to 800 pixels wide on desktop does not automatically shrink to fit a 390-pixel phone viewport. It overflows the container and breaks the layout below it. Break point three: the hamburger menu doesn't open. On mobile, the full navigation bar collapses into a hamburger menu — three horizontal lines in the top corner. The AI generates the icon, but the JavaScript that opens the menu when you tap it is sometimes missing or broken. Tap it and see if anything happens. Identify which of these three your site has before you write the fix prompt.

artifactLines: Break 1 — Hero text overflows: heading sized for 1200 px desktop extends past the right edge of a 390 px phone screen.
Break 2 — Images don't scale: fixed-width project images overflow the mobile container and break the layout below them.
Break 3 — Hamburger menu doesn't open: navigation collapses to a three-line icon but the tap/click action does nothing.
props.sparkLine: Identify which ones your site has before writing the fix prompt.

beats.act: how to ask the AI to fix each
beats.narration_text: Send all three fixes in one prompt — not three separate messages. The AI has the full context of your site in the conversation; sending one prompt is faster and less likely to introduce new inconsistencies. Here is the fix prompt. If your site only has one or two of the three break points, remove the parts that don't apply — but keep the rest of the phrasing, because it is specific enough for the tool to act on without guessing.

props.segment: Fix mobile layout — all three break points
props.command: Fix these three mobile layout issues: (1) Hero heading overflows on mobile — make the font size responsive so it fits within the phone viewport width without horizontal scroll; (2) Project images overflow on mobile — set them to max-width: 100% and width: 100% so they scale down to fit the container; (3) Hamburger menu doesn't open on mobile — ensure the click/tap event handler is wired up and the menu opens and closes correctly. Test all fixes at 390 px viewport width.

beats.act: publish and get the live link
beats.narration_text: After the mobile fixes look correct in the preview, publish. Five steps, and they are the same on every platform. Step one: find the Publish button. In v0.dev it is labeled Deploy. In Framer it is Publish. In Wix it is Publish. In Squarespace it is Publish. Look for it in the top-right corner of the editor — it is always prominently placed because it is the most important action you can take. Step two: click it and confirm. Some platforms ask you to choose a subdomain or URL slug — use your brand name in lowercase with hyphens, no spaces. Step three: wait. The platform takes ten to sixty seconds to build and push the site. Step four: copy the live URL that appears. This is the URL you submit — not the editor URL, not the preview URL, not the URL from your browser's address bar while you were editing. The live URL is a different address. Step five: open the live URL on your actual phone — not in a browser tab on your desktop. Confirm that the page loads and the three sections you fixed look correct on the real device.

artifactLines: Step 1 — Find Publish: top-right corner of the editor (labeled Deploy on v0.dev, Publish on all others).
Step 2 — Click and confirm: choose a URL slug using your brand name in lowercase with hyphens.
Step 3 — Wait: 10–60 seconds for the platform to build and push the live site.
Step 4 — Copy the live URL: this is the URL you submit — NOT the editor URL or preview URL.
Step 5 — Open on your actual phone: confirm the live site loads and mobile layout is intact on the real device.
props.sparkLine: The live URL is the deliverable — not the preview.

beats.narration_text: Verdict for Part 2. Test mobile before calling the site done — switch to mobile preview in the editor and look for the three break points: overflowing hero text, fixed-width images, and a hamburger menu that doesn't open. Send all three fixes in one prompt. Then publish and collect the live URL — not the editor URL, not the preview URL, the live one. Open it on your actual phone after publishing. The assignment requires a live link, and the live link has to work on mobile.

artifactLines: Test mobile in the editor preview BEFORE publishing — use the phone icon or breakpoint toggle.
Fix the three break points: hero overflow, fixed-width images, broken hamburger menu.
The live URL is the deliverable — copy it from the post-publish screen, not the editor address bar.
Re-verify on your actual phone after publish — the live build can differ from the preview.

beats.narration_text: Your Turn. Open your site on your phone right now — use the mobile preview toggle in the editor, not your actual phone yet. Screenshot the Hero section. Look at it. Is the heading cut off on the right side? Are there images overflowing the screen edges? Tap the navigation hamburger icon — does a menu appear? Identify the first thing that's wrong and write it down by name. Then paste this fix prompt, removing only the lines that don't apply to your site. After the fix regenerates, run through the five publish steps: find Publish, click and confirm, wait, copy the live URL, open it on your actual phone. Paste that live URL somewhere safe — you will submit it as your website deliverable.

props.segment: Build Your Website, Part 2: Make It Not Break on Mobile
props.command: Fix these mobile layout issues: (1) Hero heading overflows on mobile — make the font size responsive so it fits within the phone viewport without horizontal scroll; (2) Project images overflow on mobile — set them to max-width: 100% and width: 100% so they scale to fit; (3) Hamburger menu doesn't open on mobile — wire up the click/tap handler so the menu opens and closes correctly. Test at 390 px viewport width.
props.runningText: test on your phone first — then paste this fix prompt…

beats.narration_text: Build Your Website, Part 2: Make It Not Break on Mobile. Liam, in for Bear.
